does: This paper demonstrates that for calibrated probabilistic evaluation of LLMs and reward models, retaining all judges in multi-judge evaluation panels produces better results than the common practice of selecting only the most accurate judges. The authors conduct experiments across four labeled pairwise-evaluation benchmarks comparing accuracy-ranked top-k judge selection against using full judge panels, measuring calibration via negative log-likelihood. They show that on RewardBench2, the full panel achieves NLL of 0.006 versus 0.013 for top-5 selection, cutting calibration error in half. The advantage holds after deduplication and against stronger subset search baselines. The core finding contradicts the intuitive heuristic of curating judges by accuracy, showing this strategy fails when the optimization target shifts from point accuracy to calibrated uncertainty quantification.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using multiple LLM judges for evaluation, extending recent approaches that aggregate multiple model-based evaluators. It responds to and challenges the implicit assumption in prior ensemble-based evaluation methods that accuracy ranking should guide judge selection. The paper connects to broader work on calibration in machine learning and proper scoring rules, adapting these classical concepts to the specific problem of aggregating noisy LLM-based judgments. It also relates to research on uncertainty quantification and probabilistic forecasting, where similar principles about retaining diverse signals have emerged. The work extends calibration techniques from traditional ML settings to the newer domain of LLM-based evaluation, where judge quality is highly variable and often uncorrelated with the specific aspects needed for well-calibrated probability estimates.
enables: This finding enables more efficient and robust evaluation pipelines for LLMs and reward models by suggesting practitioners should retain diverse judge panels rather than investing effort in careful judge curation. It opens directions for understanding which judge diversity properties matter most for calibration versus accuracy, potentially leading to principled methods for judge selection beyond accuracy metrics. The result motivates future work on joint optimization of judge selection and calibration procedures, as well as theoretical analysis of when and why diversity in weak signals outperforms concentrated strong signals under proper scoring rules. It also suggests applications to other domains using multiple imperfect evaluators, from crowdsourcing to peer review systems. The work points toward developing better uncertainty quantification methods for LLM evaluations and could inform the design of more sophisticated aggregation schemes that leverage the complementarity of diverse judge signals.
